preamble.summary: The Levites receive forty-eight cities distributed among all the tribes, including the six cities of refuge. The chapter concludes with a sweeping summary: 'Not one word of all the good promises the LORD had made to the house of Israel failed — everything came to pass.'
preamble.remarkable: The Levitical city system disperses the priestly tribe throughout all Israel — they have no contiguous territory but are present everywhere, ensuring that every part of the land has access to teaching and worship leadership. The summary statement (vv. 43-45) is the theological climax of the allotment section: God's promise is fulfilled, the land is given, rest is achieved. 'Not one word failed' (lo nafal davar, v. 45) — the verb nafal ('fall') means not one promise dropped to the ground unfulfilled.
preamble.friction: The Levitical city lists involve numerous textual variants between Joshua 21 and its parallel in 1 Chronicles 6:54-81. We followed the Joshua text and noted significant variants. The phrase lo nafal davar (v. 45, 'not one word fell') uses a physical metaphor — words stand or fall like soldiers. We rendered it 'not one word... failed' to capture the sense while noting the Hebrew image.
preamble.connections: The Levitical cities implement Numbers 35:1-8. The fulfillment declaration (v. 45) is echoed in 1 Kings 8:56 by Solomon at the Temple dedication. The city lists parallel 1 Chronicles 6:54-81. The dispersal of Levi fulfills Jacob's prophecy (Genesis 49:7) but transforms the curse into a priestly vocation.

verses.text_hebrew: וַיִּגְּשׁ֞וּ רָאשֵׁ֣י אֲב֣וֹת הַלְוִיִּ֗ם אֶל אֶלְעָזָ֣ר הַכֹּהֵן֮ וְאֶל יְהוֹשֻׁ֣עַ בִּן נוּן֒ וְאֶל רָאשֵׁ֛י אֲב֥וֹת הַמַּטּ֖וֹת לִבְנֵ֣י יִשְׂרָאֵ֑ל׃
verses.text_kjv: Then came near the heads of the fathers of the Levites unto Eleazar the priest, and unto Joshua the son of Nun, and unto the heads of the fathers of the tribes of the children of Israel;
verses.rendering: The heads of the Levite ancestral houses approached Eleazar the priest, Joshua son of Nun, and the heads of the ancestral houses of the Israelite tribes.
translator_notes: The Levites approach the same leadership trio (Eleazar, Joshua, tribal heads) who oversaw all the land allocations. Their claim is presented publicly and formally, not as a private petition. This fulfills Numbers 35:1-8, where the LORD instructed that the Levites receive cities and surrounding pasturelands from each tribe's inheritance.

verses.text_hebrew: וַיְדַבְּר֤וּ אֲלֵיהֶם֙ בְּשִׁלֹ֔ה בְּאֶ֥רֶץ כְּנָ֖עַן לֵאמֹ֑ר יְהוָה֙ צִוָּ֣ה בְיַד מֹשֶׁ֔ה לָ֣תֶת לָ֤נוּ עָרִים֙ לָשֶׁ֔בֶת וּמִגְרְשֵׁיהֶ֖ן לִבְהֶמְתֵּֽנוּ׃
verses.text_kjv: And they spake unto them at Shiloh in the land of Canaan, saying, The LORD commanded by the hand of Moses to give us cities to dwell in, with the suburbs thereof for our cattle.
verses.rendering: They addressed them at Shiloh in the land of Canaan: "The LORD commanded through Moses that we be given cities to live in, along with their pasturelands for our livestock."
translator_notes: Migrasheihen ('their pasturelands/common lands') — the Levites do not receive agricultural territory but cities with surrounding open land for grazing. The migrash is a belt of open land extending outward from the city wall (Numbers 35:4-5 specifies 1,000 cubits). The Levites' request appeals entirely to Mosaic authority — Yahweh tsivvah v'yad Mosheh ('the LORD commanded by the hand of Moses'). Their claim rests on divine instruction, not negotiation.

verses.text_hebrew: וַיִּתְּנ֣וּ בְנֵי יִשְׂרָאֵ֗ל לַלְוִיִּם֙ מִנַּ֣חֲלָתָ֔ם אֶל פִּ֥י יְהוָ֖ה אֶת הֶעָרִ֥ים הָאֵ֖לֶּה וְאֶת מִגְרְשֵׁיהֶֽן׃
verses.text_kjv: And the children of Israel gave unto the Levites out of their inheritance, at the commandment of the LORD, these cities and their suburbs.
verses.rendering: So the Israelites gave the Levites these cities with their pasturelands from their own inheritance, in accordance with the LORD's command.
translator_notes: Min-nachalatam ('from their inheritance') — each tribe contributes from its own allotted territory. The Levites receive a distributed inheritance rather than a contiguous one. This arrangement embeds the priestly and teaching tribe throughout the entire nation, ensuring that instruction in the Torah is accessible to all Israel, not concentrated in a single region.

key_terms.semantic_range: inheritance, allotted possession, hereditary portion, patrimony
key_terms.note: The Levites' paradox: they receive cities 'from the inheritance' of others because their own nachalah is the LORD himself (Joshua 13:33). Their distributed allotment ensures the priestly tribe is woven throughout the nation rather than isolated in one territory.

verses.text_hebrew: וַיֵּצֵ֨א הַגּוֹרָ֜ל לְמִשְׁפְּחֹ֣ת הַקְּהָתִ֗י וַיְהִ֡י לִבְנֵ֣י אַהֲרֹ֣ן הַכֹּהֵ֣ן מִן הַלְוִיִּם֩ מִמַּטֵּ֨ה יְהוּדָ֜ה וּמִמַּטֵּ֣ה הַשִּׁמְעֹנִ֗י וּמִמַּטֵּ֤ה בִנְיָמִן֙ בַּגּוֹרָ֔ל עָרִ֖ים שְׁלֹ֥שׁ עֶשְׂרֵֽה׃
verses.text_kjv: And the lot came out for the families of the Kohathites: and the children of Aaron the priest, which were of the Levites, had by lot out of the tribe of Judah, and out of the tribe of Simeon, and out of the tribe of Benjamin, thirteen cities.
verses.rendering: The lot fell to the Kohathite clans. The descendants of Aaron the priest — who were Levites — received by lot thirteen cities from the tribes of Judah, Simeon, and Benjamin.
translator_notes: The Kohathites are divided into two groups: the priestly line (descendants of Aaron) and the non-priestly Kohathites. Aaron's descendants receive cities closest to Jerusalem (from Judah, Simeon, and Benjamin), positioning the priestly families near the future temple site — though this significance would only become apparent centuries later under David and Solomon.

verses.text_hebrew: וְלִבְנֵ֤י קְהָת֙ הַנּ֣וֹתָרִ֔ים מִמִּשְׁפְּחֹ֥ת מַטֵּה אֶפְרַ֛יִם וּמִמַּטֵּה דָ֖ן וּמֵחֲצִ֣י מַטֵּ֣ה מְנַשֶּׁ֑ה בַּגּוֹרָ֖ל עָרִ֥ים עָֽשֶׂר׃
verses.text_kjv: And the rest of the children of Kohath had by lot out of the families of the tribe of Ephraim, and out of the tribe of Dan, and out of the half tribe of Manasseh, ten cities.
verses.rendering: The remaining Kohathites received by lot ten cities from the clans of the tribe of Ephraim, the tribe of Dan, and the half-tribe of Manasseh.
translator_notes: The non-priestly Kohathites receive cities from the central tribes, extending Levitical presence through the Joseph territory and into the coastal region near Philistine territory (Dan).

verses.text_hebrew: וְלִבְנֵ֣י גֵרְשׁ֗וֹן מִמִּשְׁפְּחֹ֣ת מַטֵּה יִשָּׂשכָר֩ וּמִמַּטֵּ֨ה אָשֵׁ֜ר וּמִמַּטֵּ֤ה נַפְתָּלִי֙ וּמֵחֲצִ֨י מַטֵּ֤ה מְנַשֶּׁה֙ בַּבָּשָׁ֔ן עָרִ֖ים שְׁלֹ֥שׁ עֶשְׂרֵֽה׃
verses.text_kjv: And the children of Gershon had by lot out of the families of the tribe of Issachar, and out of the tribe of Asher, and out of the tribe of Naphtali, and out of the half tribe of Manasseh in Bashan, thirteen cities.
verses.rendering: The Gershonites received by lot thirteen cities from the clans of the tribe of Issachar, the tribe of Asher, the tribe of Naphtali, and the half-tribe of Manasseh in Bashan.
translator_notes: The Gershonite cities are distributed through the northern and northeastern tribes, ensuring Levitical presence in the Galilee region and the Transjordan Bashan.

verses.text_hebrew: לִבְנֵ֣י מְרָרִ֗י לְמִשְׁפְּחֹתָ֛ם מִמַּטֵּ֥ה רְאוּבֵ֛ן וּמִמַּטֵּה גָ֖ד וּמִמַּטֵּ֣ה זְבוּלֻ֑ן עָרִ֖ים שְׁתֵּ֥ים עֶשְׂרֵֽה׃
verses.text_kjv: The children of Merari by their families had out of the tribe of Reuben, and out of the tribe of Gad, and out of the tribe of Zebulun, twelve cities.
verses.rendering: The Merarites, by their clans, received twelve cities from the tribe of Reuben, the tribe of Gad, and the tribe of Zebulun.
translator_notes: The Merarites receive the southern Transjordan and one northern tribe (Zebulun). The three Levite clans — Kohath, Gershon, Merari — together receive 48 cities (13 + 10 + 13 + 12), exactly the number prescribed in Numbers 35:7.

verses.text_hebrew: וַיִּתְּנ֨וּ בְנֵ֤י יִשְׂרָאֵל֙ לַלְוִיִּ֔ם אֶת הֶעָרִ֥ים הָאֵ֖לֶּה וְאֶת מִגְרְשֵׁיהֶ֑ן כַּאֲשֶׁ֥ר צִוָּ֛ה יְהוָ֖ה בְּיַ֥ד מֹשֶֽׁה בַּגּוֹרָֽל׃
verses.text_kjv: And the children of Israel gave by lot unto the Levites these cities with their suburbs, as the LORD commanded by the hand of Moses.
verses.rendering: The Israelites gave these cities with their pasturelands to the Levites by lot, just as the LORD had commanded through Moses.
translator_notes: Ka'asher tsivvah Yahweh b'yad Mosheh ba-goral ('just as the LORD had commanded by the hand of Moses, by lot') — the lot (goral) ensures the distribution is perceived as divinely directed rather than humanly negotiated. The summary statement frames the entire Levitical allocation as obedience to prior divine command.

verses.text_hebrew: וַיִּתְּנ֗וּ מִמַּטֵּ֛ה בְּנֵ֥י יְהוּדָ֖ה וּמִמַּטֵּ֣ה בְנֵ֣י שִׁמְע֑וֹן אֵ֚ת הֶעָרִ֣ים הָאֵ֔לֶּה אֲשֶׁר יִקְרָ֥א אֹתָ֖ם בְּשֵֽׁם׃
verses.text_kjv: And they gave out of the tribe of the children of Judah, and out of the tribe of the children of Simeon, these cities which are here mentioned by name,
verses.rendering: From the tribe of Judah and the tribe of Simeon they gave the following cities, listed here by name.
translator_notes: The detailed city-by-city listing begins with the priestly Aaronite cities from Judah and Simeon. This specificity ensures the allocation is a matter of permanent public record, not a vague promise.

verses.text_hebrew: וַיְהִי֙ לִבְנֵ֣י אַהֲרֹ֔ן מִמִּשְׁפְּח֥וֹת הַקְּהָתִ֖י מִבְּנֵ֣י לֵוִ֑י כִּ֥י לָהֶ֛ם הָיָ֖ה הַגּוֹרָ֥ל רִאשֹׁנָֽה׃
verses.text_kjv: Which the children of Aaron, being of the families of the Kohathites, who were of the children of Levi, had: for theirs was the first lot.
verses.rendering: These went to the descendants of Aaron from the Kohathite clans of the Levites, because the first lot fell to them.
translator_notes: Ki lahem hayah ha-goral ri'shonah ('because the first lot was theirs') — the Aaronite priests receive priority in the allocation, consistent with their elevated status within the Levitical order.

verses.text_hebrew: וַיִּתְּנ֤וּ לָהֶם֙ אֶת קִרְיַ֨ת אַרְבַּ֜ע אֲבִ֤י הָעֲנָק֙ הִ֣יא חֶבְר֔וֹן בְּהַ֖ר יְהוּדָ֑ה וְאֶת מִגְרָשֶׁ֖יהָ סְבִיבֹתֶֽיהָ׃
verses.text_kjv: And they gave them the city of Arba the father of Anak, which city is Hebron, in the hill country of Judah, with the suburbs thereof round about it.
verses.rendering: They gave them Kiriath-arba — that is, Hebron — in the hill country of Judah, with its surrounding pasturelands. (Arba was the ancestor of Anak.)
translator_notes: Hebron serves dual duty: it is both a city of refuge (20:7) and a priestly city. The parenthetical identifying Arba as the ancestor of the Anakim connects this allocation back to Caleb's conquest of the giant inhabitants (14:12-15; 15:13-14).

verses.text_hebrew: וְאֶת שְׂדֵ֥ה הָעִ֖יר וְאֶת חֲצֵרֶ֑יהָ נָ֣תְנ֔וּ לְכָלֵ֥ב בֶּן יְפֻנֶּ֖ה בַּאֲחֻזָּתֽוֹ׃
verses.text_kjv: But the fields of the city, and the villages thereof, gave they to Caleb the son of Jephunneh for his possession.
verses.rendering: However, the open fields of the city and its surrounding villages had been given to Caleb son of Jephunneh as his possession.
translator_notes: A careful distinction: the priestly Levites receive the city of Hebron and its immediate pasturelands, while Caleb retains the agricultural fields (s'deh) and dependent villages (chatserim). This prevents a conflict between Caleb's personal inheritance (14:13-14) and the Levitical allocation. Both claims are honored simultaneously.

verses.text_hebrew: וְלִבְנֵ֣י ׀ אַהֲרֹ֣ן הַכֹּהֵ֗ן נָתְנוּ֙ אֶת עִ֣יר מִקְלַ֤ט הָרֹצֵ֙חַ֙ אֶת חֶבְר֤וֹן וְאֶת מִגְרָשֶׁ֙הָ֙ וְאֶת לִבְנָ֣ה וְאֶת מִגְרָשֶׁ֔הָ׃
verses.text_kjv: Thus they gave to the children of Aaron the priest Hebron with her suburbs, to be a city of refuge for the slayer; and Libnah with her suburbs,
verses.rendering: To the descendants of Aaron the priest they gave Hebron — the city of refuge for the killer — with its pasturelands, and Libnah with its pasturelands,
translator_notes: The text explicitly labels Hebron as ir miqlat ha-rotseach ('the city of refuge for the killer'), linking it to the institution from chapter 20. Libnah was a Canaanite royal city conquered in the southern campaign (10:29-30).

verses.text_hebrew: וְאֶת יַתִּ֖ר וְאֶת מִגְרָשֶׁ֑הָ וְאֶת אֶשְׁתְּמֹ֖עַ וְאֶת מִגְרָשֶֽׁהָ׃
verses.text_kjv: And Jattir with her suburbs, and Eshtemoa with her suburbs,
verses.rendering: Jattir with its pasturelands, Eshtemoa with its pasturelands,
translator_notes: Jattir and Eshtemoa are towns in the southern Judean highlands, both listed among Judah's cities in 15:48, 50. David would later send plunder to Eshtemoa (1 Samuel 30:28).

verses.text_hebrew: וְאֶת חֹלֹ֖ן וְאֶת מִגְרָשֶׁ֑הָ וְאֶת דְּבִ֖ר וְאֶת מִגְרָשֶֽׁהָ׃
verses.text_kjv: And Holon with her suburbs, and Debir with her suburbs,
verses.rendering: Holon with its pasturelands, Debir with its pasturelands,
translator_notes: Debir, formerly Kiriath-sepher, was captured by Othniel (15:15-17). Its inclusion as a Levitical city further transforms Canaanite urban centers into Israelite religious infrastructure.

verses.text_hebrew: וְאֶת אַ֖יִן וְאֶת מִגְרָשֶׁ֑הָ וְאֶת יֻטָּ֖ה וְאֶת מִגְרָשֶׁ֑הָ אֶת בֵּ֥ית שֶׁ֖מֶשׁ וְאֶת מִגְרָשֶׁ֑הָ עָרִ֣ים תֵּ֔שַׁע מֵאֵ֕ת שְׁנֵ֥י הַשְּׁבָטִ֖ים הָאֵֽלֶּה׃
verses.text_kjv: And Ain with her suburbs, and Juttah with her suburbs, and Bethshemesh with her suburbs; nine cities out of those two tribes.
verses.rendering: Ain with its pasturelands, Juttah with its pasturelands, and Beth-shemesh with its pasturelands — nine cities from these two tribes.
translator_notes: Beth-shemesh ('House of the Sun') is the city where the ark of the covenant will later arrive when returned by the Philistines (1 Samuel 6:12-19). Nine cities from Judah and Simeon form the core of the Aaronite priestly network.

verses.text_hebrew: וּמִמַּטֵּ֣ה בִנְיָמִ֗ן אֶת גִּבְע֖וֹן וְאֶת מִגְרָשֶׁ֑הָ אֶת גֶּ֖בַע וְאֶת מִגְרָשֶֽׁהָ׃
verses.text_kjv: And out of the tribe of Benjamin, Gibeon with her suburbs, Geba with her suburbs,
verses.rendering: From the tribe of Benjamin: Gibeon with its pasturelands, Geba with its pasturelands,
translator_notes: Gibeon — the city of the deception treaty (chapter 9) — now becomes a Levitical city, an ironic transformation. Geba will later serve as an Israelite military outpost against the Philistines (1 Samuel 13:3).

verses.text_hebrew: אֶת עֲנָתוֹת֙ וְאֶת מִגְרָשֶׁ֔הָ וְאֶת עַלְמ֖וֹן וְאֶת מִגְרָשֶׁ֑הָ עָרִ֖ים אַרְבַּֽע׃
verses.text_kjv: Anathoth with her suburbs, and Almon with her suburbs; four cities.
verses.rendering: Anathoth with its pasturelands, and Almon with its pasturelands — four cities.
translator_notes: Anathoth will later be the hometown of the prophet Jeremiah (Jeremiah 1:1; 11:21-23). Its identification as a Levitical/priestly city illuminates Jeremiah's priestly lineage.

verses.text_hebrew: כׇּל עָרֵ֥י בְנֵ֛י אַהֲרֹ֖ן הַכֹּהֲנִ֑ים שְׁלֹ֤שׁ עֶשְׂרֵה֙ עָרִ֔ים וּמִגְרְשֵׁיהֶֽן׃
verses.text_kjv: All the cities of the children of Aaron, the priests, were thirteen cities with their suburbs.
verses.rendering: The total number of cities for the descendants of Aaron the priests was thirteen cities with their pasturelands.
translator_notes: The summary count confirms thirteen priestly cities (nine from Judah/Simeon and four from Benjamin), matching the lot drawn in verse 4.

verses.text_hebrew: וּלְמִשְׁפְּח֣וֹת בְּנֵ֣י קְהָ֗ת הַלְוִיִּם֙ הַנּ֣וֹתָרִ֔ים מִבְּנֵ֖י קְהָ֑ת וַיְהִ֛י עָרֵ֥י גוֹרָלָ֖ם מִמַּטֵּ֥ה אֶפְרָֽיִם׃
verses.text_kjv: And the families of the children of Kohath, the Levites which remained of the children of Kohath, even they had the cities of their lot out of the tribe of Ephraim.
verses.rendering: The remaining Kohathite clans of the Levites received their allotted cities from the tribe of Ephraim.
translator_notes: The section now details the non-priestly Kohathite cities, beginning with those from Ephraim's territory.

verses.text_hebrew: וַיִּתְּנ֤וּ לָהֶם֙ אֶת עִ֣יר מִקְלַ֤ט הָרֹצֵ֙חַ֙ אֶת שְׁכֶ֤ם וְאֶת מִגְרָשֶׁ֙הָ֙ בְּהַ֣ר אֶפְרַ֔יִם וְאֶת גֶּ֖זֶר וְאֶת מִגְרָשֶֽׁהָ׃
verses.text_kjv: For they gave them Shechem with her suburbs in mount Ephraim, to be a city of refuge for the slayer; and Gezer with her suburbs,
verses.rendering: They gave them Shechem — the city of refuge for the killer — with its pasturelands in the hill country of Ephraim, and Gezer with its pasturelands,
translator_notes: Shechem functions as both refuge city and Levitical city, like Hebron. Its deep patriarchal history (Abraham's first altar in Canaan, Genesis 12:6; Jacob's purchase of land, Genesis 33:19) will culminate in the covenant renewal of chapter 24. Gezer was a Canaanite city whose inhabitants Ephraim failed to dispossess (16:10); its designation as a Levitical city suggests intended Israelite occupation despite the incomplete conquest.

verses.text_hebrew: וְאֶת קִבְצַ֖יִם וְאֶת מִגְרָשֶׁ֑הָ וְאֶת בֵּ֥ית חוֹרֹ֖ן וְאֶת מִגְרָשֶׁ֑הָ עָרִ֖ים אַרְבַּֽע׃
verses.text_kjv: And Kibzaim with her suburbs, and Bethhoron with her suburbs; four cities.
verses.rendering: Kibzaim with its pasturelands, and Beth-horon with its pasturelands — four cities.
translator_notes: Beth-horon (Upper and Lower) guards a critical pass from the coastal plain into the central highlands. It was the site of the Amorite rout during the southern campaign (10:10-11). Its assignment as a Levitical city positions Levites along a vital strategic corridor.

verses.text_hebrew: וּמִמַּטֵּה דָ֗ן אֶת אֶלְתְּקֵ֖ה וְאֶת מִגְרָשֶׁ֑הָ אֶת גִּבְּת֖וֹן וְאֶת מִגְרָשֶֽׁהָ׃
verses.text_kjv: And out of the tribe of Dan, Eltekeh with her suburbs, Gibbethon with her suburbs,
verses.rendering: From the tribe of Dan: Eltekeh with its pasturelands, Gibbethon with its pasturelands,
translator_notes: Gibbethon will later feature as a Philistine-held city that Israel repeatedly besieges (1 Kings 15:27; 16:15-17), highlighting the difficulty Dan faced in occupying its allotted territory.

verses.text_hebrew: אֶת אַיָּל֖וֹן וְאֶת מִגְרָשֶׁ֑הָ אֶת גַּת רִמּ֖וֹן וְאֶת מִגְרָשֶׁ֑הָ עָרִ֖ים אַרְבַּֽע׃
verses.text_kjv: Aijalon with her suburbs, Gathrimmon with her suburbs; four cities.
verses.rendering: Aijalon with its pasturelands, and Gath-rimmon with its pasturelands — four cities.
translator_notes: Aijalon is the valley where the sun stood still during the southern campaign (10:12). Its allocation as a Levitical city connects the land distribution to the conquest narrative.

verses.text_hebrew: וּמִמַּחֲצִ֣ית מַטֵּ֣ה מְנַשֶּׁ֗ה אֶת תַּעְנָ֖ךְ וְאֶת מִגְרָשֶׁ֑הָ וְאֶת גַּת רִמּ֖וֹן וְאֶת מִגְרָשֶׁ֑הָ עָרִ֖ים שְׁתָּֽיִם׃
verses.text_kjv: And out of the half tribe of Manasseh, Tanach with her suburbs, and Gathrimmon with her suburbs; two cities.
verses.rendering: From the half-tribe of Manasseh: Taanach with its pasturelands, and Gath-rimmon with its pasturelands — two cities.
translator_notes: Taanach lies near Megiddo in the Jezreel Valley, an area of strategic importance throughout Israelite history. The second Gath-rimmon here may reflect a textual variant; some scholars suggest this should read 'Ibleam' based on the parallel in 1 Chronicles 6:70.

verses.text_hebrew: כׇּל עָרִ֥ים עֶ֖שֶׂר וּמִגְרְשֵׁיהֶ֑ן לְמִשְׁפְּח֥וֹת בְּנֵ֥י קְהָ֖ת הַנּוֹתָרִֽים׃
verses.text_kjv: All the cities were ten with their suburbs for the families of the children of Kohath that remained.
verses.rendering: The total for the remaining Kohathite clans was ten cities with their pasturelands.
translator_notes: Ten cities for the non-priestly Kohathites, matching the count in verse 5.

verses.text_hebrew: וְלִבְנֵ֣י גֵרְשׁ֗וֹן מִמִּשְׁפְּחֹ֣ת הַלְוִיִּם֩ מֵחֲצִ֨י מַטֵּ֤ה מְנַשֶּׁה֙ אֶת עִ֣יר מִקְלַ֤ט הָרֹצֵ֙חַ֙ אֶת גּוֹלָ֤ן בַּבָּשָׁן֙ וְאֶת מִגְרָשֶׁ֔הָ וְאֶת בְּעֶשְׁתְּרָ֖ה וְאֶת מִגְרָשֶׁ֑הָ עָרִ֖ים שְׁתָּֽיִם׃
verses.text_kjv: And unto the children of Gershon, of the families of the Levites, out of the other half tribe of Manasseh they gave Golan in Bashan with her suburbs, to be a city of refuge for the slayer; and Beeshterah with her suburbs; two cities.
verses.rendering: To the Gershonites from the Levitical clans: from the half-tribe of Manasseh they gave Golan in Bashan — the city of refuge for the killer — with its pasturelands, and Be-eshterah with its pasturelands — two cities.
translator_notes: Golan in Bashan serves as both refuge and Levitical city (cf. 20:8). Be-eshterah (also called Ashtaroth) was formerly the capital of King Og of Bashan (12:4; 13:12, 31). Its transformation from a pagan royal seat (the name likely derives from the goddess Ashtoreth) into a Levitical city is theologically significant.

verses.text_hebrew: וּמִמַּטֵּ֣ה יִשָּׂשכָ֗ר אֶת קִשְׁי֖וֹן וְאֶת מִגְרָשֶׁ֑הָ אֶת דׇּבְרַ֖ת וְאֶת מִגְרָשֶֽׁהָ׃
verses.text_kjv: And out of the tribe of Issachar, Kishon with her suburbs, Dabareh with her suburbs,
verses.rendering: From the tribe of Issachar: Kishion with its pasturelands, Daberath with its pasturelands,
translator_notes: Daberath sits at the base of Mount Tabor in the Jezreel Valley, an area that will feature prominently in the Deborah-Barak narrative (Judges 4:6, 12).

verses.text_hebrew: אֶת יַרְמ֖וּת וְאֶת מִגְרָשֶׁ֑הָ אֶת עֵ֥ין גַּנִּ֖ים וְאֶת מִגְרָשֶׁ֑הָ עָרִ֖ים אַרְבַּֽע׃
verses.text_kjv: Jarmuth with her suburbs, Engannim with her suburbs; four cities.
verses.rendering: Jarmuth with its pasturelands, and En-gannim with its pasturelands — four cities.
translator_notes: En-gannim ('spring of gardens') — this is a different city from the Jarmuth listed among the five Amorite kings defeated in chapter 10 (which was in Judah's territory).

verses.text_hebrew: וּמִמַּטֵּ֣ה אָשֵׁ֗ר אֶת מִשְׁאָ֖ל וְאֶת מִגְרָשֶׁ֑הָ אֶת עַבְד֖וֹן וְאֶת מִגְרָשֶֽׁהָ׃
verses.text_kjv: And out of the tribe of Asher, Mishal with her suburbs, Abdon with her suburbs,
verses.rendering: From the tribe of Asher: Mishal with its pasturelands, Abdon with its pasturelands,
translator_notes: Asher's territory lies along the northern Mediterranean coast. These Levitical cities position priestly-teaching families in a region with heavy Canaanite and Phoenician cultural influence.

verses.text_hebrew: אֶת חֶלְקָ֖ת וְאֶת מִגְרָשֶׁ֑הָ וְאֶת רְחֹ֖ב וְאֶת מִגְרָשֶׁ֑הָ עָרִ֖ים אַרְבַּֽע׃
verses.text_kjv: Helkath with her suburbs, and Rehob with her suburbs; four cities.
verses.rendering: Helkath with its pasturelands, and Rehob with its pasturelands — four cities.
translator_notes: Rehob appears as one of the cities Asher failed to fully dispossess (Judges 1:31), suggesting that Levitical occupation of these northern cities may have been tenuous.

verses.text_hebrew: וּמִמַּטֵּ֣ה נַפְתָּלִ֗י אֶת עִ֣יר מִקְלַ֤ט הָרֹצֵ֙חַ֙ אֶת קֶ֤דֶשׁ בַּגָּלִיל֙ וְאֶת מִגְרָשֶׁ֔הָ וְאֶת חַמֹּ֥ת דֹּ֖אר וְאֶת מִגְרָשֶׁ֑הָ וְאֶת קַרְתָּ֖ן וְאֶת מִגְרָשֶׁ֑הָ עָרִ֖ים שָׁלֽוֹשׁ׃
verses.text_kjv: And out of the tribe of Naphtali, Kedesh in Galilee with her suburbs, to be a city of refuge for the slayer; and Hammothdor with her suburbs, and Kartan with her suburbs; three cities.
verses.rendering: From the tribe of Naphtali: Kedesh in Galilee — the city of refuge for the killer — with its pasturelands, Hammoth-dor with its pasturelands, and Kartan with its pasturelands — three cities.
translator_notes: Kedesh in Galilee is the third western refuge city (cf. 20:7). Its name ('holy place') suggests a pre-Israelite sacred site now incorporated into the Levitical system. Kedesh will later be the home of Barak (Judges 4:6) and the site of Israelite defeat by Tiglath-pileser III of Assyria (2 Kings 15:29).

verses.text_hebrew: כׇּל עָרֵ֥י הַגֵּרְשֻׁנִּ֖י לְמִשְׁפְּחֹתָ֑ם שְׁלֹ֤שׁ עֶשְׂרֵה֙ עָרִ֔ים וּמִגְרְשֵׁיהֶֽן׃
verses.text_kjv: All the cities of the Gershonites according to their families were thirteen cities with their suburbs.
verses.rendering: The total number of cities for the Gershonite clans was thirteen cities with their pasturelands.
translator_notes: Thirteen Gershonite cities, matching the count in verse 6.

verses.text_hebrew: וּלְמִשְׁפְּח֞וֹת בְּנֵ֣י מְרָרִ֗י הַלְוִיִּם֙ הַנּ֣וֹתָרִ֔ים מֵאֵ֖ת מַטֵּ֣ה זְבוּלֻ֑ן אֶת יׇקְנְעָ֖ם וְאֶת מִגְרָשֶׁ֑הָ אֶת קַרְתָּ֖ה וְאֶת מִגְרָשֶֽׁהָ׃
verses.text_kjv: And unto the families of the children of Merari, the rest of the Levites, out of the tribe of Zebulun, Jokneam with her suburbs, and Kartah with her suburbs,
verses.rendering: To the Merarite clans — the remaining Levites — from the tribe of Zebulun: Jokneam with its pasturelands, Kartah with its pasturelands,
translator_notes: The Merarite section completes the three-part Levitical distribution. Jokneam guards the southern approach to the Jezreel Valley, a strategically vital location.

verses.text_hebrew: אֶת דִּמְנָ֖ה וְאֶת מִגְרָשֶׁ֑הָ אֶת נַהֲלָ֖ל וְאֶת מִגְרָשֶׁ֑הָ עָרִ֖ים אַרְבַּֽע׃
verses.text_kjv: Dimnah with her suburbs, Nahalal with her suburbs; four cities.
verses.rendering: Dimnah with its pasturelands, and Nahalal with its pasturelands — four cities.
translator_notes: Nahalal is listed among the Canaanite cities that Zebulun failed to drive out, instead subjecting them to forced labor (Judges 1:30).

verses.text_hebrew: וּמִמַּטֵּ֣ה רְאוּבֵ֗ן אֶת בֶּ֖צֶר וְאֶת מִגְרָשֶׁ֑הָ וְאֶת יַ֖הְצָה וְאֶת מִגְרָשֶֽׁהָ׃
verses.text_kjv: And out of the tribe of Reuben, Bezer with her suburbs, and Jahazah with her suburbs,
verses.rendering: From the tribe of Reuben: Bezer with its pasturelands, Jahaz with its pasturelands,
translator_notes: Bezer is the Transjordan city of refuge (20:8). Jahaz was the site of Israel's decisive battle against Sihon king of the Amorites (Numbers 21:23; Deuteronomy 2:32).

verses.text_hebrew: אֶת קְדֵמ֖וֹת וְאֶת מִגְרָשֶׁ֑הָ וְאֶת מֵיפָ֖עַת וְאֶת מִגְרָשֶׁ֑הָ עָרִ֖ים אַרְבַּֽע׃
verses.text_kjv: Kedemoth with her suburbs, and Mephaath with her suburbs; four cities.
verses.rendering: Kedemoth with its pasturelands, and Mephaath with its pasturelands — four cities.
translator_notes: Kedemoth was the staging area from which Moses sent emissaries to Sihon (Deuteronomy 2:26).

verses.text_hebrew: וּמִמַּטֵּה גָ֗ד אֶת עִ֣יר מִקְלַ֤ט הָרֹצֵ֙חַ֙ אֶת רָאמֹ֤ת בַּגִּלְעָד֙ וְאֶת מִגְרָשֶׁ֔הָ וְאֶת מַחֲנָ֖יִם וְאֶת מִגְרָשֶֽׁהָ׃
verses.text_kjv: And out of the tribe of Gad, Ramoth in Gilead with her suburbs, to be a city of refuge for the slayer; and Mahanaim with her suburbs,
verses.rendering: From the tribe of Gad: Ramoth in Gilead — the city of refuge for the killer — with its pasturelands, Mahanaim with its pasturelands,
translator_notes: Mahanaim ('two camps') is where Jacob encountered angels (Genesis 32:2) and where Ish-bosheth will establish his rival kingdom (2 Samuel 2:8) and David will flee during Absalom's rebellion (2 Samuel 17:24). Its assignment as a Levitical city weaves patriarchal history into the national infrastructure.

verses.text_hebrew: אֶת חֶשְׁבּ֖וֹן וְאֶת מִגְרָשֶׁ֑הָ אֶת יַעְזֵ֖ר וְאֶת מִגְרָשֶׁ֑הָ כׇּל עָרִ֖ים אַרְבַּֽע׃
verses.text_kjv: Heshbon with her suburbs, Jazer with her suburbs; four cities in all.
verses.rendering: Heshbon with its pasturelands, and Jazer with its pasturelands — four cities in all.
translator_notes: Heshbon was the capital of King Sihon (Numbers 21:25-26; Joshua 12:2). Like Be-eshterah/Ashtaroth, a former enemy capital is now repurposed as a Levitical city.

verses.text_hebrew: כׇּל הֶעָרִ֗ים לִבְנֵ֤י מְרָרִי֙ לְמִשְׁפְּחֹתָ֔ם הַנּוֹתָרִ֖ים מִמִּשְׁפְּח֣וֹת הַלְוִיִּ֑ם וַיְהִ֥י גוֹרָלָ֖ם עָרִ֥ים שְׁתֵּ֥ים עֶשְׂרֵֽה׃
verses.text_kjv: So all the cities for the children of Merari by their families, which were remaining of the families of the Levites, were by their lot twelve cities.
verses.rendering: The total number of cities for the Merarite clans — the remaining Levitical families — was twelve cities by their lot.
translator_notes: Twelve Merarite cities, matching the count in verse 7. The grand total across all three Levitical clans: 13 (Aaronite) + 10 (other Kohathites) + 13 (Gershonites) + 12 (Merarites) = 48 cities.

verses.text_hebrew: כֹּ֚ל עָרֵ֣י הַלְוִיִּ֔ם בְּת֖וֹךְ אֲחֻזַּ֣ת בְּנֵ֣י יִשְׂרָאֵ֑ל עָרִ֛ים אַרְבָּעִ֥ים וּשְׁמֹנֶ֖ה וּמִגְרְשֵׁיהֶֽן׃
verses.text_kjv: All the cities of the Levites within the possession of the children of Israel were forty and eight cities with their suburbs.
verses.rendering: The total number of Levitical cities within the territory held by the Israelites was forty-eight cities with their pasturelands.
translator_notes: Forty-eight Levitical cities exactly matches the number commanded in Numbers 35:7. This correspondence demonstrates Israel's faithfulness in executing the Mosaic legislation regarding the Levites.

verses.text_hebrew: תִּהְיֶ֜ינָה הֶעָרִ֥ים הָאֵ֛לֶּה עִ֥יר עִ֖יר וּמִגְרָשֶׁ֣יהָ סְבִיבֹתֶ֑יהָ כֵּ֖ן לְכׇל הֶעָרִ֥ים הָאֵֽלֶּה׃
verses.text_kjv: These cities were every one with their suburbs round about them: thus were all these cities.
verses.rendering: Each of these cities included its surrounding pasturelands; this was the arrangement for every one of these cities.
translator_notes: A formulaic closing note confirming that the standard arrangement — city plus surrounding pasturelands — applied uniformly to all forty-eight Levitical cities.

verses.text_hebrew: וַיִּתֵּ֤ן יְהוָה֙ לְיִשְׂרָאֵ֔ל אֶת כׇּל הָאָ֔רֶץ אֲשֶׁ֥ר נִשְׁבַּ֖ע לָתֵ֣ת לַאֲבוֹתָ֑ם וַיִּרָשׁ֖וּהָ וַיֵּשְׁב֥וּ בָֽהּ׃
verses.text_kjv: And the LORD gave unto Israel all the land which he sware to give unto their fathers; and they possessed it, and dwelt therein.
verses.rendering: The LORD gave Israel the entire land he had sworn to give their ancestors. They took possession of it and settled in it.
translator_notes: This climactic summary (vv. 43-45) is one of the most theologically significant passages in Joshua. Vayyirashuhah vayyeshvu vah ('they took possession of it and settled in it') — the twin verbs yarash ('possess, dispossess') and yashav ('settle, dwell') mark the fulfillment of the promise trajectory that began with Abraham in Genesis 12:7. The statement is programmatic rather than absolute — it declares the essential fulfillment of the land promise even though some areas remain unconquered (cf. 13:1-7; 15:63; 16:10; 17:12-13).

verses.text_hebrew: וַיָּ֨נַח יְהוָ֤ה לָהֶם֙ מִסָּבִ֔יב כְּכֹ֥ל אֲשֶׁר נִשְׁבַּ֖ע לַאֲבוֹתָ֑ם וְלֹא עָ֤מַד אִישׁ֙ בִּפְנֵיהֶ֔ם מִכׇּל אֹיְבֵיהֶ֑ם אֵ֚ת כׇּל אֹיְבֵיהֶ֔ם נָתַ֥ן יְהוָ֖ה בְּיָדָֽם׃
verses.text_kjv: And the LORD gave them rest round about, according to all that he sware unto their fathers: and there stood not a man of all their enemies before them; the LORD delivered all their enemies into their hand.
verses.rendering: The LORD gave them rest on every side, exactly as he had sworn to their ancestors. Not one of their enemies could stand against them; the LORD handed all their enemies over to them.
translator_notes: Vayyanach Yahweh lahem missaviv ('the LORD gave them rest round about') — the verb nuach ('to rest') echoes the purpose statement of Deuteronomy 12:10 and 25:19. Rest (menucha) is the goal of the entire exodus-conquest narrative: God's people dwelling securely in God's land under God's rule. Lo amad ish bifneihem ('not a man stood before them') — this bold claim, read alongside the incomplete-conquest notices, affirms divine faithfulness to the covenant promises. Military opposition has been broken even where demographic displacement is incomplete.

verses.text_hebrew: לֹ֣א נָפַ֗ל דָּבָר֙ מִכֹּל֙ הַדָּבָ֣ר הַטּ֔וֹב אֲשֶׁ֥ר דִּבֶּ֛ר יְהוָ֖ה אֶל בֵּ֣ית יִשְׂרָאֵ֑ל הַכֹּ֖ל בָּֽא׃
verses.text_kjv: There failed not ought of any good thing which the LORD had spoken unto the house of Israel; all came to pass.
verses.rendering: Not a single word failed from all the good promises the LORD had spoken to the house of Israel. Everything was fulfilled.
translator_notes: Lo nafal davar mikkol ha-davar ha-tov ('not one thing fell from all the good word') — nafal ('fell') is used for the failure or dropping of a word. The assertion is absolute: every good thing God promised has come to pass. This verse is the thematic pivot of the entire book, the bridge between the conquest/allocation narrative (chs. 1-21) and the farewell/warning section (chs. 22-24). Joshua will repeat this claim in his farewell speech (23:14) but add the sobering corollary: just as every good promise was fulfilled, so every threat of judgment will be fulfilled if Israel disobeys.
